explanation: The formula 'G(prop_0 -> prop_1)' means that globally (at all times), if prop_0 holds, then prop_1 must also hold at that same time. This matches the requirement that whenever prop_0 is true, prop_1 should also be true.
This means whenever prop_0 holds, then prop_1 must also hold at the same time (globally). In LTL, we encode this as 'globally (if prop_0 then prop_1)'.
The requirement is that whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must also hold. In LTL, this can be globally expressed as: globally, if prop_0 is true, then prop_1 must also be true.

This formula states that at every point in time, if prop_0 holds, then prop_1 must also hold at that point. This captures the meaning of the conditional 'If prop_0, then prop_1.' in linear temporal logic.

The statement 'If prop_0, then prop_1' expresses that whenever prop_0 is true, prop_1 must also be true. In LTL, this is captured by the globally operator applied to the implication: G (prop_0 -> prop_1).
The statement means that at all points in time, if prop_0 holds, then prop_1 must also hold at the same point. In LTL, this is globally (G) (prop_0 -> prop_1).
The statement says that whenever prop_0 holds, then prop_1 must also hold. In LTL, this is usually expressed as 'globally, if prop_0 then prop_1'.
This means that whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must also hold at the same time. In LTL this is usually captured using globally: at all times, if prop_0 then prop_1.
This LTL formula represents that whenever prop_0 is true, prop_1 must also be true at that moment. This is a global implication.
This is a safety property stating that whenever prop_0 holds true, prop_1 must also hold at the same time. This should always be true at every point in time, so it is globally required.
